title: How Particles Tunnel Through Solid Walls

lesson.learn:
Quantum tunneling is one of the strangest effects in physics. In the classical world, if a particle does not have enough energy to cross a barrier, it stops. In quantum mechanics, the particle is described by a wave function, and that wave can extend through barriers. As a result, there is a real probability that the particle appears on the other side.

That does not mean the particle is cheating physics. It means the quantum world is probabilistic in a way classical intuition does not prepare us for.
lesson.simpler: Tiny particles can sometimes pass through barriers they should not be able to cross. That is called quantum tunneling.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Quantum tunneling happens because particles behave like waves with a chance of appearing beyond a barrier.
lesson.deeperDive:
Tunneling matters in nuclear fusion, scanning tunneling microscopes, radioactive decay, and many modern technologies. The barrier does not disappear. Instead, the wave function decreases inside it, leaving a nonzero chance of detection on the other side. That tiny probability can be enough when the system repeats over and over.

Quantum tunneling is a perfect example of why the microscopic world is not intuitive. A wall is not always a wall to a quantum particle. Sometimes it is more like a challenge with a very small but real chance of success.
lesson.funFact: The Sun's fusion reactions depend partly on tunneling.
references: National Institute of Standards and Technology - scanning tunneling microscopy

people: Quantum physicists - researchers who study tunneling
Nuclear physicists - scientists who use tunneling in fusion
Engineers - people who build tunneling microscopes
places: The Sun - a natural place where tunneling matters
Semiconductor devices - where tunneling affects electronics
Microscope setups - where tunneling is used for imaging
years: 21st century - tunneling remains crucial in nanotech
Present day - it is taught as a core quantum phenomenon
concepts: Wave function - the quantum description of a particle
Probability amplitude - the quantity that determines outcomes
Barrier penetration - passing through a forbidden region
Classically forbidden - impossible under ordinary physics
Quantum behavior - rules that differ from everyday intuition
